coredata.dc:title: A step forward toward quantum dots based perovskite solar cells in an ambient environment

Shaikh, Ahson Jabbar
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of complete quantum dot based hole and electron transport layers for perovskite solar cells is carried out with different compositions of hybrid electron transport layers including carbon and inorganic based quantum dots, in an ambient environment. The experimental efficiency of cells is plotted against the percentage composition of quantum dots in electron transport layer, and unknown efficiencies of other compositions of electron transport layer are estimated by extrapolating the graph, with a minimal set-up of only three devices. This makes it a smart minimal set-up design that extrudes maximum information in a minimum time period for a range of perovskite solar cells. The efficiency of solar cells is associated with the LUMO levels of electron transport layers including pure and hybrid materials, where the LUMO is calculated by different methods including density-functional theory calculations. This smart formulation will allow us to use minimal setups in the future to characterize multiple perovskite solar cells simultaneously.

head.ce:title: A step forward toward quantum dots based perovskite solar cells in an ambient environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of complete quantum dot based hole and electron transport layers for perovskite solar cells is carried out with different compositions of hybrid electron transport layers including carbon and inorganic based quantum dots, in an ambient environment. The experimental efficiency of cells is plotted against the percentage composition of quantum dots in electron transport layer, and unknown efficiencies of other compositions of electron transport layer are estimated by extrapolating the graph, with a minimal set-up of only three devices. This makes it a smart minimal set-up design that extrudes maximum information in a minimum time period for a range of perovskite solar cells. The efficiency of solar cells is associated with the LUMO levels of electron transport layers including pure and hybrid materials, where the LUMO is calculated by different methods including density-functional theory calculations. This smart formulation will allow us to use minimal setups in the future to characterize multiple perovskite solar cells simultaneously.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are well known for their bandgap to be tuned by controlling their size, which allows them to have enhanced light-harvesting properties [1,2], hence improved efficiency of solar cells. Few examples of these QDs that have been used in perovskite solar cells include SnO2 QDs [3], CdS QDs [1], CdSe QDs [4], PbS QDs [5], CuS QDs [6] ZnO QDs, and GQDs [7] etc. Hence quantum dots as either electron transport layer (ETL) or hole transport layer (HTL) are a good choice for the fabrication of perovskite solar cells (PSCs).

One major limitation for the commercialization of perovskite solar cells is that high efficiency solar cells have only been reported to be fabricated in an inert Argon or Nitrogen atmosphere, which constitutes industrial scale-up problems, e.g., an inverted PSC fabricated under ambient open air condition gave a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 9.9% which is a 22.66% decrease in PCE as compared to that made under N2 [8]. Recently, researchers have focused their attention to the possibility of fabrication of PSCs in an ambient open-air environment with few successful results e.g., a PCE of 15.56% was obtained when Cs0.15FA0.85PbI3 PSC was fabricated in ambient air with great thermal and light stability, which also shows great potential for large scale manufacturing and commercialization [1,9]. Other researchers have demonstrated that PCE of PSCs depend on the relative humidity, for instance up to 10% PCE can be attained when the relative humidity is between 15 and 25%. However, the PCE of the PSCs was decreased to 3.63%, when relative humidity was increased to 40–50%, and a further decrease of 0.35% in PCE was observed when the relative humidity was further increased to 60–70% [10]. The fabrication of PSCs under ambient air condition is still under its developmental phase.

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) having interesting optical properties e.g., down conversion [11], strong UV absorbance, and high fluorescence emission intensity etc.; along with their ability to functionalize other semiconductors materials have made them an important material for the fabrication of solar cells with enhanced efficiencies [12]. GQDs as interlayers between titanium oxide and perovskite improves the extraction of electrons from perovskite layers and their injection into the TiO2 layer [13,14]. The doping of TiO2 with GQDs improves the performance and stability of perovskite solar cells [15], whereas blending of GQDs with P3HT and MEH-PPV polymers, and SnO2 QDs also enhances the efficiency of photovoltaics devices [16,17]. A layer of GQDs coated on Si to form a GQDs/Si heterojunction solar cell also enhanced the PCE from 6.56% to 12.35%, where the GQDs role was to transport holes [18].

Inorganic zinc oxide (ZnO) quantum dots are semiconductor material with wide band gap and have higher electron mobility as compared to TiO2, which makes them suitable for electron transport layer (ETL) [19,20]. ZnO can easily be solution-processed at low temperatures and can be applied on flexible substrate PSCs with an average PCE reaching upto 11.9% [21]. A further higher PCE of upto 18.1% can be achieved when ZnO is doped with indium to fabricate flexible PSCs, owing to higher open circuit voltage (Voc) and fill factor (FF) [22]. Further, ZnO also serves as a better contact layer between the perovskite layer and the electrode layer, and has an advantage of blocking holes due to its lower HOMO level. ZnO based devices are also known for their stability, where higher PCE of 16.6% was achieved and the device retained 85% of its output efficiency even after 1000 h [23].

Similarly, copper sulfide (CuS) QDs have also received tremendous attention recently owing to their applications in photovoltaic devices, gas sensors, catalysis and non-linear optics etc. [24]. CuS QDs are readily available HTL material which are low cost, solution processable and soluble in common solvents that enhances the efficiency of organic solar cells and provide remarkable efficiency, even better than traditional solution processable PEDOT:PSS HTL [25]. A CuS QDs HTL based inverted planar heterojunction perovskite solar cell device has shown better PCE, when compared to the device without CuS QDs [24,26]. Other nanoscale phases of copper sulfide e.g., Cu2S have also been used in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) as barrier layer [27].

The need to eliminate charge carrier recombination has underlined the importance of hybrid ETL and HTL materials since it is important for the LUMO level of ETLs to align with the perovskite layer for effective electron extraction, likewise, the HOMO level for the efficient hole extraction. Hybrid ETLs allow us to achieve desirable band alignment of ETL/perovskite heterojunction [17], that serves to remove current leakage and charge carrier recombination for efficient transport of electrons and holes [28]. Other benefits that can be achieved are enhanced electron extraction and transport capability which improves the overall performance of PSCs, optimized stability and efficiency [17,29–31]. The hybrid ETL is also reported to have enhanced crystalline structure of the photoactive perovskite layer [28], and surface protection to the perovskite layer from the atmosphere [32], also resulting in improved efficiency and long term stability.

So far, to the best of our knowledge, complete QD based solar cells are not reported yet, which we consider worthwhile to investigate, especially in an ambient environment as a step forward toward commercialization and as a cost-effective method. We used a combination of carbon quantum dots (GQDs) and inorganic quantum dots (ZnO QDs) as hybrid materials for electron transport layer and chalcogenide copper sulfide quantum dots as hole transport material, which resulted in a complete QD based ETL and HTL device, prepared in an ambient open air environment associated with lower costs, quick processing and analysing of data. A mixed iodide-bromide methyl ammonium lead perovskite (MAPbIBr2)- type material was used because its band gap was compatible with GQDs band gap for effective electron extraction. Finally, extrapolating the efficiencies of known devices resulted in finding approximate efficiencies of unknown combination of ETLs, including pure materials as well. This resulted in a smart minimal set-up design of three devices only for achieving maximum information for perovskite solar cells.

The chemicals used for the synthesis of electron and hole transport materials, and for the fabrication of perovskite solar cells were obtained commercially. Acetone (C3H6O), methanol (CH3OH), and sodium sulfide trihydrate (Na2S·3H2O) were purchased from BDH chemicals. Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), potassium hydroxide (KOH), 1-butanol (C4H10O), trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7·2H2O), copper chloride dihydrate (CuCl2·2H2O), dimethyl sulfoxide (C2H6OS), dimethylformamide (C3H7NO), zinc acetate dihydrate (ZnC4H6O4·2H2O), isopropyl alcohol (C3H8O), and chlorobenzene (C6H5Cl) were purchased from Daejung, Korea. Lead bromide (PbBr2), methyl ammonium iodide (MAI), silver metal (Ag), and fluorine-doped tin oxide glass (FTO-glass) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received, without any further purification steps.

GQDs were prepared by the method described in the literature using citric acid as a starting material precursor [33]. 3g of citric acid was weighed in small beaker and then heated at 180 °C until the white citric acid crystals melted. The heating was continued until there was a change in colour from colourless to yellow and lastly orange within 25–30 min. A 1 M NaOH solution was prepared by dissolving 4g NaOH in 100 mL distilled water, which was added in drops to the melted dense orange liquid obtained via pyrolysis of citric acid while stirring until the pH approached to neutral. The obtained solution was dried on rotary evaporator at 50 °C and the powdered GQDs obtained were stored for future use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of zinc oxide quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of ZnO quantum dots was followed by using the literature method with minor modifications [34]. 3g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 100 mL of methanol and stirred under reflux. A solution of 1.5g of KOH was dissolved in methanol (65 mL), which was added drop-by-drop to the solution of zinc acetate dihydrate over a duration of 15 min, which was further stirred for 2.5 h under reflux conditions. Heating was stopped and mixture was then allowed to cool down at room temperature. Precipitates were formed, supernatant liquid was decanted, and the precipitates were washed twice with methanol and then with 1-butanol respectively using centrifuge [34]. The precipitates were then dried and calcined for 3 h at 500 °C in a furnace to obtain ZnO quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of copper sulfide quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CuS QDs solution was performed according to the method reported by Zhou et al. [35]. 100 mL of 1 mmol aqueous solution of CuCl2·2H2O (0.0170 g) and 0.68 mmol of trisodium citrate dihydrate (0.02 g) was stirred at room temperature, into which 1 mL of 1 M sodium sulfide (Na2S) solution was added slowly. A pale-blue solution of the mixture turned dark brown immediately. 5 min later, the reaction mixture was heated to 90 °C and stirred for further 15 min. The mixture turned to dark green indicating the completion of the reaction and formation of CuS QDs. This CuS QDs solution was dried on rotary evaporator at 80 °C to obtain green powdered CuS QD nanoparticles.
The size and structure of these prepared materials was characterized by UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, FTIR, DLS, SEM, and XRD.

The GQDs-ZnO QDs thin films were spin-coated on the FTO glass, followed by perovskite layer, and then the copper sulfide quantum dots were deposited on top of the perovskite layer. Finally silver electrode was deposited using thermal evaporator to obtain a standard device architecture of FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs/MAPbIBr2/CuS QDs/Ag- electrode. The electrical properties of the solar cells were characterized and the efficiency was observed using solar simulator. For our devices, it was possible to extrapolate the PCE of pure GQDs and pure ZnO QDs from the data points of PCEs of the different concentration ratio of GQDs and ZnO QDs in our hybrid GQDs-ZnO QDs ETLs, by plotting a graph of PCE of solar cells vs percentage of GQDs in the hybrid ETL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Etching and cleaning of FTO-coated glass
ce:section.ce:para: The FTO-coated glass was etched by removing the FTO coating from two-fifth of the surface area from one edge using sandpaper and a potentiometer (TES 2360 LCR Multimeter) was used to ensure the etched area is non-conducting. The etched glass was then washed with detergent for 10 min using sonicator in order to remove dust particles, then rinsed with distilled water for 10 min to remove traces of detergents. It was then sonicated in acetone for 10 min to remove organic contaminants and then it was finally cleaned in isopropyl alcohol (IPA) for another 10 min to remove metallic impurities [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ETL and HTL solutions
ce:section.ce:para: The stock solutions of GQDs and ZnO QDs were prepared in deionized water with a concentration of 10 mg/mL each, where as CuS QDs solution was prepared in chlorobenzene with a concentration of 10 mg/mL. The hybrid ETL GQD-ZnO QD hybrids solution was prepared in the ratios of 20:80, 50:50, and 60:40 v/v respectively for these three devices.

The perovskite precursor was prepared according to literature protocols [36,37]. Shortly, 367 mg of lead bromide (PbBr2) was dissolved in 1 mL of DMF and 10 mg of methylammonium iodide (MAI) was dissolved in 1 mL of DMSO. The two solutions were mixed and stirred for 30 min. PC-351 Corning hot plate stirrer was used for this purpose.

1 mL of GQD-ZnO QD (20:80) hybrid solution was spin coated as an electron transporting layer on the FTO glass. The spin coater was operated at 1500 rpm for a period of 10 s and then at 3000 rpm for 20 s. The sample was annealed on a hotplate heated at 100 °C for 10 min. This procedure was also carried out for the 50:50 and 60:40 hybrids. VTC-100 vacuum spin coater was used for this purpose. The schematics for spin coating of these hybrids is provided in the supporting information of this article.

Samples of FTO glass coated with ETL from the above procedure were then allowed to cool at room temperature and then placed back on the spin coater. 1 mL of the perovskite was spin coated on top of the GQDs-ZnO QDs layer at 1500 rpm for 10 s and then at 3000 rpm for 20 s and then was carefully annealed on the hotplate at 100 °C. A change in colour to a burnt orange colour indicated the formation of perovskite crystals. The schematics for spin coating of perovskite layer is provided in the supporting information of this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spin coating of CuS QDs HTL layer
ce:section.ce:para: The devices from the preceeding step were allowed to cool and placed on the spin coater and 1 mL solution of CuS QDs was spin coated at 1500 rpm for 10 s and 3000 rpm for 20 s respectively. Finally these samples were annealed at 100 °C to form CuS thin film. The schematics for spin coating of this layer are provided in the supporting information of this article.

In this final step, the three devices were masked using aluminum foil, leaving only a small exposed area for the silver (Ag) to be deposited and placed above the silver metal in the thermal evaporator. 99.9% pure silver was deposited on the CuS QDs layer as a cathode for all three devices using thermal evaporator operating at approximately 10−6 mbar. The schematics for silver deposition is also shown in the supporting information of this article.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–visible spectroscopy of individual and hybrid quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (SPECORD 200 PLUS by Analytik Jena, Germany) having a combination of halogen and deuterium lamp with fixed spectral bandwidth was used for UV–Vis analysis of the synthesized materials. This instrument has an operational wavelength range from 190 to 1100 nm with a wavelength accuracy of ±0.1 nm and a lamp change set at 320 nm. The samples to be measured were placed in a quartz cuvette of 1 cm path length and the absorption spectrum of GQDs, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs were measured. For GQDs, the stock solution was diluted with distilled water (1:15) and the UV absorption peak was observed. For hybrids, stock solutions of 10 mg/mL for both GQDs and ZnO QDs were prepared and further diluted with distilled water (1:15) to give a final concentration of 0.625 mg/mL for pure GQDs and pure ZnO QDs. GQDs-ZnO QDs (20:80) hybrid was prepared by titrating 200 μL of GQDs with 800 μL of ZnO QDs. Similarly, GQDs-ZnO QDs (50:50) hybrid was prepared by titrating 500 μL of GQDs with 500 μL of ZnO QDs. Finally, GQDs-ZnO QDs (60:40) hybrid was prepared by titrating 600 μL of GQDs with 400 μL of ZnO QDs respectively.

In order to examine the bandgap of GQDS, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs, we plotted a graph of (αhv)2 (eV2cm−2) vs. energy (eV) of the photon using the Tauc's relation given in equation (1) below

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence spectroscopy of individual and hybrid quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence spectroscopy is a highly sensitive and selective tool used for the detection of optical properties and the electronic interactions of materials. It stands as a suitable technique to study the relationship between the fluorescence change (enhancement or quenching) and concentration of analyte from the resulting Stern-Volmer plots. HORIBA FluoroMax-4 spectrophotometer was used for the fluorescence spectroscopy measurements at room temperature. These measurements were taken by using a quartz cuvette that was transparent from all four sides with a fixed pathlength of 10 mm. The slit-width was fixed to 2 mm with a bandpass of 8.5 nm for all the experiments. This technique was used for characterizing the fluorescence of GQDs, ZnO QDs, CuS QDs and GQD-ZnO hybrids for titration studies. The samples for fluorescence were prepared by combining the correct amounts and appropriate ratios from stock solutions of graphene quantum dots and ZnO QDs in water, details of which are provided in the table given in supplementary information. For pure ZnO QDs and CuS QDs, a 1 mL solution of the 0.01 M stock solutions was further diluted in 2.5 mL of distilled water, and the samples were excited at a wavelength of 265 nm. For binding studies [39,40]. The concentration of GQDs were kept constant at 7.64 × 10−4 M, while increasing the concentration of ZnO QDs from 0 μM to 347 μM.

A computer-controlled Bruker Tensor 27 Fourier-transform infrared spectrometer equipped with ZnSe ATR and software version Opus 65 was used to characterize the prepared samples of GQDs, ZnO QDs, and CuS QDs. The range was observed from 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1.

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and Zeta-potential (ξ) measurements were carried out at 25 °C using Zetasizer Nano ZSP from Malvern Panalytical Technologies, UK equipped with the Malvern Zetasizer software (v7.13). These DLS measurements were carried out by diluting 1 mL (10 mg/mL) of GQDs, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs solutions each 9 mL of distilled water to make 10 mL of solution. The size measurements were performed by using 173° non-invasive backscatter mode and the zeta potential was observed by M3-phase analysis light scattering mode, respectively. This instrument has a red 10.0 mW 633 nm He − Ne laser. The accuracy of this instrument is better than ±2% on NIST traceable latex standards. The multiple peak high-resolution fitting procedure was used to obtain the particle size distribution from the autocorrelation function. The intensity distribution curves for GQDs, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs were considered for particle size distribution.

The zeta potential measurements were taken from the same solutions as used for the DLS measurements. Disposable folded capillary cells (DTS1070) were pre-rinsed with distilled water (∼10 mL) and then filled with samples by using a 5 mL disposable syringe, while ensuring that no bubbles were present inside the cell. Triplicates of each sample were measured for size and zeta potential for accuracy purposes. Zeta potential graphs, their standard values along with standard deviation is provided in the SI of this paper.

The high-performance JOEL JSM-6490 scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to analyze the surface morphology, and to estimate the size of the quantum dots. The instrument has a magnification up to 100,000×, with a high spatial resolution of up to 3.0 nm, which allow surfaces to be observed with fine details. Our samples were analyzed with 20–30 kV accelerating voltage at 40,000× magnification with a scale bar of 500 nm. The samples were prepared by diluting them 10-fold, placing a little amount on FTO glass to cover it completely, removing the left-over liquid after 5 min so that selected number nanoparticles are observed only, and were air dried before imagery.

For X-Ray diffraction studies, XRD: STOE, Germany, Scintag Theta-Theta X-ray Diffractometer was used to confirm the crystalline structure of prepared graphene, zinc oxide and copper sulfide quantum dots. The scanning range selected was 20°–80°.

Keithley source meter (Model M2420) was used to measure the J-V characteristics by sweeping the voltage from −1.1 V to 1.1 V, set at 60 sweep points for all the three devices. The performance of perovskite solar cells was measured by using solar simulator (Sciencetech AM 1.5G Filter Solar Simulator) with the lamp illumination power intensity of 100 mW/cm2, calibrated using a reference standard silicon photovoltaic cell (JIM-100). The solar simulator was generating standard AM1.5 solar spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene quantum dots, zinc oxide quantum dots and copper sulfide quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: The size of a nanoparticle impacts greatly on the properties of materials. The optical properties of nanoparticles are usually studied using UV–Vis spectroscopic technique which measures the absorption band. Fig. 1 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of prepared GQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid. This UV–Vis spectrum provides a characteristic absorption peak at 340 nm, which agrees closely to earlier reported result [13]. This absorption is a result from a n→π* transition of C═O in the UV region, due to absorption of light by electrons in the non-bonding molecular orbital (n) and their characteristic excitation to higher energy anti-bonding molecular orbital (that is, π* state), and a long tail that extends into the visible region, which can be ascribed to absorption shoulders emanating from a π→π* transition of the C═C bonds. GQDs showed an optical band gap of 5.33 eV as estimated from Tauc's plot (see Fig. 1b).

The UV–Vis absorption peak of ZnO QDs solution showed a sharp, horn-like peak at 373 nm (Fig. 1c), indicating the formation of ZnO QDs. This peak and its sharpness also indicates the characteristic wurtzite hexagonal structure of ZnO QDs, and formation of monodispersed nanosized ZnO QDs [41]. The optical band gap of ZnO QDs derived using the Tauc's plot was obtained to be 4.59 eV as shown in Fig. 1d.

CuS QDs shows a moderate broad absorption peak in the near-infrared (NIR) region between 900 nm and 1000 nm [52,53]. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CuS QDs is presented in Fig. 1e, showing a broad, well-defined absorption peak in the NIR region at 941 nm. This characteristic absorption peak is a result of localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR). Mostly, copper-based chalcogenides whose composition is not copper-rich, have the highest energy state of their valence band unoccupied and the free holes in this empty energy state give rise to the LSPR absorption band in the NIR region [42]. The optical band gap energy of the synthesized CuS QDs was found to be 4.6 eV (Fig. 1f), having absorption in the visible light region as well. CuS QDs has a well defined low band gap which accounts for its useful application in optoelectronic devices [43–47] with HOMO value of −5.1 eV which is very suitable for hole collection from the perovskite layer in perovskite solar cells [26,47].

Fig. 2 a shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of the prepared diluted solution (1:15) of GQDs in water. When excited at an excitation wavelength of 265 nm, a strong fluorescent peak appeared at 341 nm which indicated that GQDs are highly fluorescent in solution. Depending on the pH and excitation wavelength, different photoluminescent (PL) peaks of varying intensities, can be observed [33]. Exciting the GQDs at a shorter wavelength means greater excitation energy of photons is made available for effective excitation of GQDs. The non-zero bandgap of GQDs is a result of their relatively small size and quantization of aromatic skeletal framework [33].

The photoluminescence (PL) of ZnO QDs shows two characteristic peaks at 336 nm and 379 nm respectively as represented in Fig. 2b. This PL is due to the quantum confinement effect which changes the physical properties of ZnO QDs at nanoscale, where the bandgap energy of ZnO QDs is increased. The peak at 336 nm is attributed to the excitonic near-band-edge (NBE) emission of ZnO and the other peak at 379 nm is deep-level-emission (DLE) due to the vacancies of singly ionized oxygen species. This emission is caused by the recombination of a hole generated by a photon with an electron in the ionized oxygen vacancy [41,48].

CuS QDs are one of the most useful metal chalcogenide semiconductors owing to their high fluorescent quantum yield, narrow and size-dependent emission wavelength, and an extensive excitation band. The CuS QDs solution was excited at 265 nm wavelength and gave an intense fluorescent emission peak at 337 nm as shown in Fig. 2c. Emission peaks at higher excitation wavelength are possible as have been reported by other researchers [49,50]. CuS QDs usually give emission band in the near band-edge region which is due to the variation in the composition of Cu and S. It is important to point out that the information from the fluorescence emission spectrum of CuS QDs can be used to distinguish different stable phases of copper sulfide [51–53]. Nevertheless, the broad and intense peak of CuS QDs in this work illustrates high fluorescent properties that made CuS QDs a fascinating luminescent semiconductor material for solar cell applications as hole transport layer.

The FT-IR spectroscopic characterization of GQDs represented in Fig. 3 a showed a broad absorption peak characteristic of –OH functional group at 3478 cm−1. The bands at 3000 cm−1 and 2930 cm−1 are consistent with stretching vibration of = C–H and –C–H groups respectively. Absorption at 2310 cm−1 is due to CO2 absorbed from the sample atmosphere. The carbonyl functional group (CO) has a sharp prominent peak at 1654 cm−1. The reduction of the band wavenumber by magnitude up to 45 cm−1 is due to the presence of α, β unsaturated double bond in the carbonyl system. Similarly, CC absorption is lowered to 1450 cm−1. The alkoxy group appeared around 1297 cm−1 for the C–O–C stretching vibrations. The peak at 1091 cm−1 shows the stretching vibrations of C–OH groups. These observed peaks confirmed the formation of graphene quantum dots [54].

The FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 3b shows the absorption spectrum of ZnO QDs. The peak at 3425 cm−1 represents the –OH group which is due to the bending vibration of absorbed water molecules as well as the stretching vibration of –OH group. The weak peak at 2344 cm−1 is consistent with CO2 band, which must have been absorbed from the sample's surface. The fingerprint region of the spectrum gave an absorption band at 486 cm−1 which represents Zn–O vibrations [55,56].

The FT-IR spectrum of CuS QDs represented in Fig. 3c shows traces of –OH band at 3440 cm−1 which is due to the bending vibration of the –OH group. The absorption of CO2 from the surface of the samples provided a weak peak at 2348 cm−1. A characteristic absorption peak at 614 cm−1 corresponds to Cu–S stretching vibrations of Cu2S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta (ζ) potential studies
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrodynamic size of the synthesized GQDs was calculated by using dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. The hydrodynamic diameter of the particles shown in Fig. 4 a is based upon their diffusion via Brownian motion in solution and not the actual particle size, hence the z-average of the prepared GQDs was found to be 102 nm, which is the average of the core GQDs, ions and solvent molecules moving with this polar moiety. As a matter of fact, the actual size of these GQDs is less than 10 nm, however DLS generally represent GQDs much larger in size [57]. The average electrokinetic potential at the slipping plane (ζ-potential) gave a value of −2.35 mV, which indicates that the GQDs synthesized are relatively neutral, since their value falls between ±10 mV. However, these particles are probably not stable in solution and have tendency to agglomerate with time as stable particles have zeta potential values greater than +30 mV or less than −30 mV [58]. However, practically, in standard room temperature conditions, these GQDs were not agglomerated and showed stability for a longer period of time upto 12 months.

The hydrodynamic size of the prepared ZnO QDs measured using DLS technique is shown in Fig. 4b, providing a value of 164 nm. This value also does not depict the actual size of the ZnO quantum dots, but also includes the ions and solvent molecules surrounding the core nanoparticle, which move along with it in the solution. The zeta potential of ZnO QDs shows that the particle is relatively neutral in solution as its value of −8.52 mV lies within the range for a neutral colloidal suspension, however experimentally, these particles are quite stable in solution.

Fig. 4c represents the hydrodynamic diameter distribution of CuS QDs, for which a value of 116 nm was recorded, which is also greater than the size of the nanoparticles. The zeta potential of CuS QDs was found to be −34.2 mV. This shows that the CuS QDs are highly dispersed in solution with exceptional stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) studies
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 shows scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images at 40,000 magnification each, at a scale of 500 nm for GQDs, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs respectively. Their corresponding particle size distribution graphs are also shown. The average size of GQDs was calculated to be 9.22 ± 5.11 nm, the size of ZnO QDs was calculated to be 15.67 ± 10.04 nm and CuS QDs particle size was calculated to be 4.71 ± 2.12 nm respectively.

Fig. 6 a shows the XRD pattern of GQDs, which matches with the standard pattern (ICSD 01-075-1621) [38]. The peak at 2θ value of 26.83° corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphite, this implies that pyrolysis of citric acid produces a structure that is characteristic of graphite [59]. The XRD pattern of ZnO QDs is shown in Fig. 6b, displaying a crystalline structure that conforms to the hexagonal wurtzite structure having diffraction peaks reflected at 2θ values corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), (202), (104), and (203) crystal planes respectively. This data was similar to the ZnO nanoparticles with the reference ICSD code of 01-079-0206 [60,61]. The hexagonal structure of CuS QDs was matched to the crystallographic data (ICSD 00-001-1281) [62], giving diffraction peaks at 2θ values that correspond to (101), (102), (103), (006), (105), (110), (108), (201), (203) and (116) crystallographic planes respectively, as shown in Fig. 6c and is also in agreement with earlier reported data in the literature [46,63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding studies of GQD-ZnO QD hybrids
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 a shows UV–Vis absorption spectrum of GQD-ZnO hybrids in different ratios with increasing concentration of GQDs and decreasing concentration of ZnO QDs [64–66]. The difference between the individual peak of pure GQDs and pure ZnO QDs from the peak of the hybrids indicates that binding has occurred between GQDs and ZnO QDs. The stock solutions for GQDs and ZnO QDs were prepared for the UV–Vis analysis of hybrids. The hybrids were prepared in the same ratio as were used for the solar cells, reported in the next section. However, the concentration was kept lower for the UV–Vis analysis, as higher concentration is not suitable for UV–Vis analysis. The ratios of GQDs:ZnO QDs were 20:80, 50:50, and 60:40 respectively. The differences observed between the individual peaks of pure GQDs and pure ZnO QDs from the peaks of the hybrids indicated that increasing the concentration of GQDs and decreasing the concentration of ZnO QDs continuosly lowered the absorbance peak. Since this does not show any kind of interaction between these two moities, therefore two additional experiments (Fig. 7b and c) were carried out. In one of the experiments, the GQDs were kept constant and in another one, ZnO QDs were kept constant. In this last UV–Vis experiment, it was evident that both moities are interacting with each other, which was due to first increase in the absorption and then after reaching equilibrium, a decrease in the UV–Vis absorbance was observed. This indicated that binding has occurred between GQDs and ZnO QDs, and hybrids are formed. However, these UV–Vis results do not provide enough information about the binding parameters, therefore these results were complimented by fluorescence studies for additional information.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is a highly sensitive and selective tool used for the detection of optical properties and electronic interaction of materials. This technique stands as a suitable technique to study the relationship between the fluorescence enhancement and concentration of analyte from the resulting Stern-Volmer plots.

The Stern-Volmer equations are given as:
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K a
[ Q ]
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
where F0 represents fluorescence emission in the absence of quencher, that is, the pure substance; and F is the fluorescence emission in the presence of analyte or quencher. Ka and Ksv are the binding constant for static and dynamic quenching respectively and [Q] represent the concentration of quencher. Fig. 7d shows the fluorescence emission spectrum of GQD-ZnO QD hybrids. In this experiment, the concentration of GQDs was kept constant at 7.64 × 10−4 M, while increasing the concentration of ZnO QDs from 0 μM to 347 μM. Pure GQDs solution and the hybrids were excited at the same wavelength of 265 nm. It was desirable to use shorter wavelength for fluorescence experiment, as shorter wavelength implies greater energy of photons which is more effective for excitation. Further, the intensity of fluorescence decreases as excitation wavelength increases, which made us to select excitation wavelength at 265 nm. The fluorescence emission spectrum of GQD-ZnO QD hybrids shows a fluorescence enhancement with increased intensity as the concentration of ZnO QDs increases. This is mainly due to the fluorescent nature of ZnO QDs. Fig. 7e and f shows the Stern-Volmer plots of the two prominent peaks of GQD-ZnO QD hybrids. A downward non-linear negative curve shows photoluminescence enhancement due to binding interaction of GQDs and ZnO QDs with a binding constant of 2.0 × 103 M−1 and 2.4 × 103 M−1 and correlation coefficient corresponding to 0.9975 and 0.9958 for these two prominent peaks respectively. The first peak corresponds to GQDs, whereas the second peak corresponds to the zinc oxide quantum dots. The change in the ZnO QDs is significant at higher concentrations as more free form of ZnO QDs are present at higher concentrations as compared to the hybrids.

The binding of graphene quantum dots and zinc oxide quantum dots was also investigated by using dynamic light scattering by comparing the size and zeta potential of hybrids with the individual materials. This comparison is shown in Fig. 8 a and b, where it is clearly observed that the two moieties bind to each other, and we see a clear change in the size and zeta potential of hybrids. The observed hydrodynamic size of the hybrids is 487 nm, and zeta potential is approximately −25 mV, which shows much higher stability as compared to the individual GQDs and ZnO QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Energy level diagram of fabricated solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The selection of our materials was based on the energy levels of these materials, mainly HOMO and LUMO. We were interested in the HOMO energy level of hole transport material that is copper sulfide for the effective movement of holes, and LUMO energy level for hybrid electron transport materials that is graphene quantum dots and zinc oxide quantum dots for the effective transportation of electrons. This selection of materials and their effective energy levels along with their respective bandgaps is shown in Fig. 9 below. Further, this diagram shows that upon illumination of the device with a solar simulator, the perovskite layer absorbed incoming photons, where the electrons were excited from the HOMO level to the LUMO level, leaving the holes behind. HTL and ETL transported these holes and electrons to the cathode (Ag electrode) and anode (FTO electrode) respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: J-V characterization of fabricated solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: The J-V characteristics of FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (Fig. 10 a) based on the device architecture of FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (20:80)/CuS QDs/Ag (Fig. 10b), gave an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.54 V, a short circuit current (Jsc) of 7.32 mA/cm2, with a power conversion efficiency of 2.0%. Another FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (50:50)/CuS QDs/Ag solar cell device (Fig. 10d) show a short circuit current (Jsc) of 8.28 mA/cm2, an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.54 V, and fill factor (FF) of 51%, thereby making the device to have a power conversion efficiency of 2.3% as represented in Fig. 10c. A third device having the ratio of ETL hybrid for GQDs-ZnO QDs hybrid as 60:40 v/v (i.e., FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (60:40)/CuS QDs/Ag solar cell device), as shown in Fig. 10f, gave an open circuit voltage (Voc) of 0.54 V, a short circuit current (Jsc) of 9.34 mA/cm2, although a slightly lower fill factor (FF) of 49%, but has the highest power conversion efficiency of 2.5% (Fig. 10e). All three device produced the same maximum voltage when no load was applied to it. This shows that increasing the concentration of GQDs and decreasing the concentration of ZnO QDs have a positive effect on the efficiency of solar cells, whereas at the same time, decreasing the concentration of GQDs and increasing the concentration of ZnO QDs have a negative effect on the efficiency of solar cells. This illustrates that GQDs as ETL are more efficent as compared to inorganic ZnO QDs. However, the emphasis is on the hybrids, which allowed us to obtain these results with consistent increase in PCE with increasing concentration of GQDs.

The power conversion efficiency of pure GQDs and Pure ZnO QDs based ETLs solar cells can be calculated by plotting a graph (Fig. 11 ) of percentage composition of GQDs in GQD-ZnO QD hybrids against the efficiency of the solar cells and extrapolating the values. Accordingly, the PCE of solar cell with pure GQDs ETL at 100% concentration of GQDs gave a PCE of 3.8 and the PCE of solar cell device with pure ZnO QDs at 0% concentration of GQDs gave a PCE of 2.05, which is an intercept on the y-axis of the polynomial curve. This value shows that by functionalizing ZnO QDs with GQDs, PCE of the solar cells is increased and as the concentration of GQDs in the hybrid ETL increases, the PCE also increase.

In Fig. 9, the values for the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of GQDs, ZnO QDs and CuS QDs were extracted from the literature [7,24,67,68]. For the LUMO level of the hybrid ETL, an average of the GQDs and ZnO QDs was assumed [69]. Nonetheless, it is pertinent to calculate and estimate the HOMO-LUMO levels of the hybrids in comparison to that of pure GQDs and ZnO QDs. These calculations will clarify not only the values observed from the literature, but also explain the PCE obtained in our studies. Using the Tauc's equation, the direct band gap of GQDs-ZnO QDs was calculated from the UV–Vis result obtained in Fig. 7a and the corresponding Tauc's plot is shown in Fig. 12 . A direct band gap of 5.05 eV was calculated for GQDs-ZnO QDs (20:80) hybrid, 5.27 eV for GQDs-ZnO QDs (50:50) hybrid and 5.36 eV for GQDs-ZnO QDs (60:40) hybrid, respectively. The calculated direct band gap for GQDs and ZnO QDs is discussed earlier in Fig. 1.

The obtained band gaps were used to calculate the HOMO and LUMO level potentials using the following empirical equations.
EHOMO=χ - Ee - 0.5 Eg
ELUMO=χ - Ee + 0.5 Eg
ELUMO=EHOMO + Eg
Where, E
HOMO and E
LUMO are the HOMO and LUMO potentials, respectively. E
g is the band gap of these materials calculated from Tauc's plot as described above. E
e is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.5 eV) [70–73]. Finally, χ is the electronegativity of the nanocomposites, calculated through following equation
χ =
[
x
( A )
a
x
( B )

x
( C )

]
1
a + b + c
where, x(A), x(B), x(C) etc. are the electronegativities of individual atoms present in the composites. a, b, and c are the number of atoms present in those composites [71]. The calculated values of E
g and χ for all these materials are shown in Table 1 .

When the LUMO levels were assessed in Fig. 13 , we observed that GQDs have the highest LUMO level which confirms that the higher electron transport was better associated with the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 9 previously. Upon decreasing the concentration of GQDs and increasing the concentration of ZnO QDs, lowered LUMO levels were observed which brought the hybrid ETLs in a lesser alignment with the perovskite layer, hence the PCE was also decreased.

In order to further confirm that the LUMO energy level of GQDs-ZnO QDs was lower than the LUMO energy level of GQDs, geometry optimization was carried out using density functional theory (DFT) computations at LanL2DZ/B3LYP basis set. A representative GQDs molecule, the structure of which is provided in the supporting information of this article, containing a small part of graphene with carboxylic acid functional groups was used for these calculations. The optimized structure was further investigated for HOMO-LUMO energy levels and the band gap, using frontier molecular orbital analysis to better understand the molecular excitation and the electronic properties of the molecular systems. The LUMO orbital which is the innermost orbital that contains empty orbitals is related to electron affinity for efficient electron extraction from the perovskite layer [76]. It is worth noting, that the computational results corroborate our earlier calculations that the hybrids have lower LUMO energy level as compared to GQDs (Fig. 14 ), which explains that hybrids are relatively less aligned with the perovskite layer and hence, have an lower power conversion efficiency than GQDs [69]. The band gaps obtained for GQDs, ZnO QDs, and hybrids were 3.454 eV, 2.379 eV, and 1.646 eV respectively. These bandgaps are much lower as compared to the bandgap values obtained by Tauc's plot earlier.

Graphene quantum dots, zinc oxide quantum dots and copper sulfide quantum dots were synthesized using standard methods and characterized using various techniques that include UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering for hydrodynamic radius, zeta potential for their stability studies, SEM and XRD. Graphene quantum dots and zinc oxide quantum dots are already known materials to act as electron transport layers, whereas copper sulfide quantum dots are known as hole transport materials for the fabrication of perovskite solar cells. We intended to make use of GQD-ZnO QD hybrids as new hybrid materials as electron transport layers, while at the same time use copper sulfide quantum dots as hole transport layer, resulting in low-cost devices based on QDs only. This unique combination is reported for the first time for the fabrication of perovskite solar cells is based on the appropriate energy levels of these materials. Our main target was to obtain information on four different aspects that were considered to obtain simultaneously with a minimal experimental setup of three devices only. These aspects were (i) investigation of hybrid electron or hole transport layers, where we focused on hybrid electron transport layers in this work, (ii) fabrication of all quantum dot based materials as hole and electron transport layers applied for perovskite solar cells, considering two different categories i.e. carbon based and inorganic based quantum dots were used for electron transport layers, (iii) finding the quickest way to analyze experimental efficiency comparison of solar cells for various combinations of hybrid materials and extrapolating them to find unknown efficiencies, (iv) a step forward for the air processing of perovskite solar cells. For this purpose, GQD-ZnO QDs composites were prepared in the same ratio for UV–Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, DLS for hydrodynamic size and zeta potential for stability measurements. Higher concentrations in the same ratio were used later for the fabrication of solar cells. The first experiments showed that UV–Vis absorption of hybrids was decreasing with increasing concentration of GQDs and decreasing concentration of ZnO QDs. However, additional careful UV–Vis experiments revealed that interactions occur between these two moieties, by observing increase and then decrease of absorption of these hybrids, differed by the equilibrium point. The peak of zinc oxide was dominant in all cases. The interesting point was hybrids were showing much higher absorption than individual quantum dots. Further, fluorescence experiments showed increase in fluorescence intensity with increase in the concentration of ZnO quantum dots. We considered both the fluorescence peaks of ZnO QDs and calculated the corresponding Stern-Volmer plots, where we were able to abstract the binding efficiency of these two classes of materials to form hybrids, which came at an average value of 2.2 × 103 M−1. The calculated binding efficiency was considered as an additional information about the binding parameters between these two classes of quantum dots. The size and zeta potential clearly shows that not only hybrids are formed, but also these are much stable then individual materials. Finally, three different solar cell devices having the device architectures of FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (20:80)/CuS QDs/Ag; FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (50:50)/CuS QDs/Ag; and FTO/GQDs-ZnO QDs (60:40)/CuS QDs/Ag were fabricated. Their J-V characteristics showed similar values for open circuit voltage (Voc) i.e., 0.54 V; however, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of these devices were calculated to be 2.0%, 2.3%, and 2.5% respectively. When these values were plotted in a graph, we were able to extrapolate the values for pure GQDs only, pure ZnO QDs only and any combination in between for these devices. Through the Tauc's plot, we calculated the bandgap, which then allowed us to find the HOMO-LUMO energy levels of these materials, confirming that LUMO levels of GQDs are more aligned with the perovskite layer as compared to the hybrids or ZnO QDs. These calculations were further verified using density-functional theory calculations, which also showed that GQDs LUMO levels are higher as compared to hybrids or ZnO QDs, hence better aligned with the perovskite layer. The better alignment of these GQDs makes them better electron transport layer as compared to ZnO QDs, resulting in a better PCE. Overall, we were able to achieve information on all four aspects described above and obtained maximum information in a minimal set-up of only three devices. We are extremely hopeful that such smart formulations will allow us to use minimal setups in the future to characterize multiple perovskite solar cells simultaneously.
Data sharing not applicable to this article as no datasets were generated or analyzed during the current study.

Afzal Hussain Kamboh: Solar Cell Fabrication. Ahson Jabbar Shaikh: Conceptualization, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Supervision, Writing- Reviewing and Editing, Validation. ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

The supplementary information includes schemes for the synthesis of GQDs, ZnO QDs, and CuS QDs; schematics for the spin coating of GQDs-ZnO QDs layer, MAPbIBr2 layer, CuS QDs layer, and Ag layer as cathode; zeta potential graphs for all three QDs and GQD-ZnO hybrids; hydrodynamic size of hybrids obtained from DLS; and the approximate structure of GQDs used for the computational analysis. Finally, a table containing the details for the titration experiments is also provided.
